# COVID-19 and the HLH Paradigm

The COVID-19 pandemic, caused by SARS-CoV-2 infection, provided an unprecedented natural experiment in hyperinflammatory disease at a global scale [@Ciotti2020The]. Early in the pandemic, the striking clinical overlap between severe COVID-19 and secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) prompted a paradigm-shifting proposal: that clinicians should screen critically ill COVID-19 patients for HLH using the HScore [@Mehta2020COVID]. Published as a correspondence in *The Lancet* in March 2020 and subsequently accruing over 7,500 citations, this letter by Mehta and colleagues fundamentally reframed how the critical care community understood COVID-19-associated organ failure, positioning it within the broader framework of cytokine storm syndromes.

## Phenotypic Overlap with Secondary HLH

The clinical similarities between severe COVID-19 and classical secondary HLH are substantial. Both conditions feature hyperferritinemia, a hallmark of macrophage activation that serves as both a biomarker and a pathogenic mediator of tissue damage [@Kernan2017Hyperferritinemia]. Serum ferritin levels exceeding 10,000 ng/mL, once considered almost pathognomonic for HLH, were documented in severe COVID-19 patients with progressive respiratory failure. Interleukin-6 (IL-6) elevation emerged as a central feature of COVID-19 severity, mirroring its established role in HLH-associated cytokine cascades [@Tanaka2016Immunotherapeutic; @McGonagle2020The]. Macrophage activation, documented through pathological studies of lung tissue and bronchoalveolar lavage, revealed that dysregulated monocyte and macrophage responses were key drivers of COVID-19 immunopathology [@Merad2020Pathological]. Multi-organ failure, the final common pathway, occurred through mechanisms recognizable to clinicians experienced in managing HLH: hepatic dysfunction, cytopenias, coagulopathy, and hemodynamic instability [@Ragab2020The].

These overlapping features were not merely superficial. The cytokine storm framework, previously confined to the hematology and rheumatology literature, became the dominant explanatory model for COVID-19 severity in mainstream critical care discourse [@Moore2020Cytokine; @Soy2020Cytokine]. Webb and colleagues developed clinical criteria specifically for COVID-19-associated hyperinflammatory syndrome, adapting HLH diagnostic principles to the pandemic context [@Webb2020Clinical].

## Critical Immunological Differences

Despite these phenotypic similarities, careful immunological characterization revealed that COVID-19-associated hyperinflammation is not classical HLH. The most critical distinction lies in the interferon-gamma (IFN-gamma) axis. In classical HLH, particularly genetic forms, IFN-gamma is massively elevated and drives the pathological macrophage activation loop---a finding validated by the therapeutic success of emapalumab, an anti-IFN-gamma monoclonal antibody [@Locatelli2020Emapalumab]. In severe COVID-19, however, IFN-gamma levels are comparatively low, and the type I interferon response is paradoxically suppressed [@Merad2020Pathological]. Similarly, interleukin-18 (IL-18), a key inflammasome-derived cytokine that is markedly elevated in macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) associated with systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis, is not characteristically elevated to the same degree in COVID-19 [@Kaplanski2018Interleukin; @Crayne2019The]. These differences suggest that COVID-19 hyperinflammation represents a related but distinct entity within the broader cytokine storm spectrum, driven more by innate immune dysregulation and monocyte-macrophage pathology than by the T-cell and natural killer cell dysfunction that defines classical HLH [@Jamilloux2020Should].

The coagulopathy of COVID-19 further distinguishes it from HLH. McGonagle and colleagues proposed that COVID-19-associated coagulopathy is primarily a pulmonary intravascular coagulopathy driven by immunothrombosis---a process in which complement activation, neutrophil extracellular traps, and endothelial damage produce localized microvascular thrombosis within the pulmonary vasculature [@McGonagle2020Immune]. This is pathologically distinct from the disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) and consumptive coagulopathy that characterizes advanced HLH, where systemic fibrinogen consumption and hypofibrinogenemia predominate. The recognition of this distinction had direct therapeutic implications, as it supported the early use of anticoagulation in COVID-19 rather than the immunosuppressive strategies typically employed for HLH-associated coagulopathy.

## Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children

The pediatric manifestation of SARS-CoV-2-associated hyperinflammation, termed multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C), provided further evidence of the virus's capacity to trigger HLH-like pathology. Verdoni and colleagues, reporting from the Italian epicenter of the epidemic, documented a 30-fold increase in the incidence of Kawasaki-like disease during the SARS-CoV-2 outbreak [@Verdoni2020An]. In their observational cohort, group 1 (n = 19, diagnosed before the epidemic) was compared with group 2 (n = 10, diagnosed during the epidemic). Children in group 2 were significantly older (mean age 7.5 versus 3.0 years), had higher rates of cardiac involvement (6 of 10 versus 2 of 19), Kawasaki disease shock syndrome (5 of 10 versus 0 of 19), and MAS (5 of 10 versus 0 of 19; all p < 0.01). Eight of ten children in group 2 tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 antibodies. Similar epidemiological patterns were documented in France [@Belot2020SARSCoVrelated], confirming that MIS-C represented a post-infectious hyperinflammatory syndrome with features overlapping both Kawasaki disease and MAS-HLH.

## Therapeutic Crossover and Lasting Legacy

The conceptual link between COVID-19 and HLH catalyzed the rapid repurposing of immunomodulatory therapies across disease boundaries. Dexamethasone, a cornerstone of HLH treatment protocols since HLH-94, was validated as the first life-saving intervention in severe COVID-19 through the RECOVERY trial, reducing 28-day mortality in patients receiving supplemental oxygen. Tocilizumab, an anti-IL-6 receptor antibody previously used for cytokine release syndrome in CAR T-cell therapy [@Kotch2019Tocilizumab; @Neelapu2018Chimeric], was repurposed for COVID-19-associated hyperinflammation. Anakinra, the IL-1 receptor antagonist with established efficacy in MAS [@Shakoory2016Interleukin; @Dinarello2018Overview], was investigated for the hyperinflammatory phenotype of COVID-19, targeting patients identified through elevated ferritin and inflammatory markers.

Perhaps the most enduring legacy of the COVID-19 and HLH intersection is the mainstreaming of HLH diagnostic frameworks into routine critical care practice. The HScore, ferritin monitoring, and the concept of cytokine storm screening---previously the domain of specialized hematologists and rheumatologists---are now standard components of the intensive care unit assessment toolkit [@Knaak2020Hemophagocytic]. This cross-pollination of expertise ensures that clinicians across disciplines are better equipped to recognize and manage hyperinflammatory syndromes, regardless of their specific trigger.
